label: quality
description:
Root cause: the concurrent renderer was scheduling low-priority updates on the same queue as user-blocking events, causing visible input lag on slow devices. The issue manifested specifically when setState was called inside a requestAnimationFrame callback — the scheduler treated it as DefaultLane instead of InputContinuousLane.

Fix: added lane detection for rAF-originated updates. Updates scheduled during a rAF now inherit the parent event's lane priority rather than defaulting to DefaultLane.

Tradeoff: adds one lane check per setState call inside rAF (~0.2µs overhead measured on V8). Acceptable given the input responsiveness improvement.

Alternative considered: batching all rAF updates into a single flush — rejected because it breaks cases where components intentionally render incrementally across frames.

Reviewers: check the lane inheritance logic at ReactFiberWorkLoop.js:L847. The edge case where multiple rAF callbacks fire before a commit needs verification.

Tested: manual testing on Pixel 3a (slow device) with CPU throttling 6x. Input lag dropped from ~180ms to ~12ms.

label: quality
description:
The useDeferredValue hook was not correctly deferring in StrictMode double-invocation. In development, React double-invokes effects and renders to detect side effects. useDeferredValue was using a ref to track the 'deferred' value, but the ref was being reset on the second invocation, making the deferred value identical to the current value on every render.

Root cause: the ref initialization in useDeferredValue used the initial value as both the current and deferred value. On double-invoke, the second call overwrote the deferred ref with the already-resolved value.

Fix: moved the deferred value tracking to use React state (via useState) rather than a ref. State survives double-invocation correctly.

Breaking behavior: useDeferredValue now causes one additional render in development (StrictMode) compared to before. Production behavior unchanged.

Reviewers: verify the transition from deferred → current value still batches correctly in concurrent mode (ReactFiberHooks.js:L1203).

label: quality
description:
Context selectors were re-rendering consumers even when the selected slice hadn't changed. The issue was in the context comparison logic: we were doing a shallow equality check on the full context object rather than on the selector output.

When a Provider updates its value, all consumers re-render regardless of what their selector returns. For large context objects (Redux-style global state), this caused O(n) re-renders on every dispatch.

Fix: memoize selector output per consumer. On context update, run selector, compare to previous selector output via Object.is. Skip re-render if selector output is identical.

Measured impact: in a benchmark app with 500 context consumers each selecting one field, update dispatch time dropped from 48ms to 3ms.

Tradeoff: selector functions must be stable references (memoized with useCallback or defined outside component). Documented this requirement in the hooks docs.

Alternative: use a separate state management library. Rejected — this fix makes React's built-in context viable for medium-complexity apps without external dependencies.

label: quality
description:
useEffect cleanup was running after the new effect on fast refresh (HMR) in development. This caused a window where the cleanup from the old render had fired but the new effect hadn't registered yet — race condition visible as a flickering subscription in React DevTools.

Root cause: fast refresh calls cleanup synchronously before triggering a re-render, but the new render is async. Gap between cleanup and re-mount.

Fix: in development with fast refresh enabled, defer cleanup until after the new effect has registered. Wrapped cleanup calls in a microtask queue flush.

Only affects development. Production cleanup timing unchanged.

Reviewers: check that the microtask deferral doesn't break tests that assert cleanup timing precisely. There are 3 test files that may need updating: ReactEffectOrdering-test.js, ReactHooksWithNoopRenderer-test.js, ReactUseEffectCleanup-test.js.

label: quality
description:
Hydration mismatches were silently swallowed in production when the mismatch occurred inside a Suspense boundary that had already resolved. The error was being caught by the Suspense error boundary and treated as a recoverable hydration error, but no warning was logged and the client silently re-rendered the subtree.

This meant SSR/client mismatches inside resolved Suspense boundaries were invisible in production monitoring.

Fix: added a separate error code (HYDRATION_MISMATCH_IN_SUSPENSE) and ensured it reaches the onRecoverableError callback even when caught by Suspense. Integrators using error monitoring (Sentry, etc.) now receive these events.

Alternative: treat as fatal hydration error. Rejected — the current fallback behavior (client re-render) is correct; we just need visibility.

Reviewers: verify onRecoverableError is called exactly once per mismatch, not once per affected node.

label: quality
description:
The App Router was not correctly invalidating the full-route cache when a Server Action mutated data that affected a layout segment above the page. The cache key for layouts is based on the segment path, not the data dependencies, so revalidatePath('/') was required to bust the layout cache even when only a nested route changed.

Root cause: the revalidation signal from Server Actions propagated downward through the route tree but not upward. Layout segments above the action's route were not included in the invalidation sweep.

Fix: changed revalidation to walk the full route tree from root when a Server Action fires, marking all segments that share a cache dependency as stale.

Performance impact: adds ~2ms to Server Action completion on route trees with >10 segments. Measured on a 50-segment route tree.

Alternative: require developers to explicitly specify which layouts to revalidate. Rejected — too much developer burden for a correctness issue.

Reviewers: test with nested layouts that have independent data fetching. The stale-while-revalidate behavior for non-affected segments should be preserved.

label: quality
description:
Parallel routes with intercepting routes were not correctly restoring scroll position on browser back navigation. When navigating forward into an intercepted route (modal), then pressing back, the scroll position was reset to 0 instead of restoring to the pre-modal position.

Root cause: the scroll restoration logic read from history.state, but the intercepted route pushed a new history entry without preserving the scroll position from the previous entry.

Fix: before pushing the intercepted route history entry, capture current scroll position and store it in the outgoing state. On popstate, read the incoming state's preserved scroll position.

Edge case: if the user scrolls inside the modal before pressing back, we restore the pre-modal position (not the modal scroll). This is the correct behavior per browser UX convention.

Reviewers: test on iOS Safari — scroll restoration has historically been unreliable there due to elastic scrolling. Manual test steps in test/e2e/parallel-routes-scroll.test.ts.

label: quality
description:
The middleware matcher was evaluated on every request including static asset requests (_next/static, _next/image, favicon.ico), adding ~3ms of overhead per static request at scale. This was documented as a known issue but never fixed because the matcher ran before the request was classified.

Fix: added a fast-path check that skips middleware evaluation for requests matching the static asset prefix pattern before the matcher is invoked. The check is a simple string prefix test (O(1)) rather than a regex.

Why not just document it: at 10k req/s, 3ms × 40% static requests = 120ms of unnecessary middleware CPU per second per instance. At scale this adds up to meaningful cost.

Breaking change: middleware can no longer intercept _next/static requests. This was never a supported use case and was explicitly warned against in docs. Added deprecation notice for anyone relying on it.

Reviewers: verify that custom static folder paths (configured via next.config.js assetPrefix) still work correctly.

label: quality
description:
generateStaticParams was fetching data in parallel for all locales but then writing the results sequentially to disk, causing a bottleneck during build that scaled linearly with locale count. For a site with 20 locales × 10k pages = 200k pages, the sequential write phase added ~8 minutes to build time.

Fix: batched the disk writes using a worker pool (existing next-swc worker infrastructure). Writes now happen in parallel up to the worker count (default: CPU count - 1).

Measured improvement on 200k page build: 14min → 6min total build time. The write phase specifically dropped from 8min to 90sec.

Tradeoff: higher peak disk I/O during build. On NFS or networked filesystems this could cause I/O saturation. Added a NEXT_BUILD_WRITE_CONCURRENCY env var to tune (default: CPU count - 1, min: 1).

Alternative: streaming writes. Rejected — adds complexity and the worker pool approach is already proven by the existing compilation pipeline.

label: quality
description:
The TypeScript language server was leaking memory when files were closed in large workspaces. Each closed file left a 'file watcher' entry in the language service's internal map that was never cleaned up. In a workspace with 10k files opened and closed over a long session, this caused the extension host process to grow to >2GB before crashing.

Root cause: the cleanup path for file close events called disposeWatcher() but the watcher entry was only removed from the map on explicit disposal, not on file close. The watcher object was deallocated but the map entry persisted with a dangling reference.

Fix: added explicit map.delete() call in the file close handler after disposeWatcher(). Added a WeakMap alternative for the secondary index to allow GC to collect entries automatically.

Verified with a 4-hour session reproducer script that opened/closed files in a loop. Memory stabilized at ~400MB (baseline) instead of growing continuously.

Reviewers: check that the WeakMap change doesn't affect the iteration pattern in rebuildOpenFilesList() which iterates over all live watchers.

label: quality
description:
The diff editor was rendering incorrect line decorations when a file had mixed line endings (CRLF + LF in the same file). The decoration offset calculation assumed uniform line endings, so after a CRLF line, all subsequent decoration positions were off by 1 character.

Root cause: the model used buffer offset for decoration positioning, but the diff computation used line+character coordinates. The conversion between them assumed LF-only endings.

Fix: updated the offset-to-position converter to account for CRLF by checking the line ending type per line rather than globally. Added a fast path for LF-only files to avoid regression.

This was particularly visible in files that had been edited on both Windows and Mac without normalization.

Reviewers: verify that the fast path (LF-only detection) doesn't add measurable overhead on the hot path for large files. The hot path is called on every cursor move.

label: quality
description:
The settings search was returning stale results after a settings sync from another device. The search index was built at startup and cached in memory, but sync events that modified settings.json did not invalidate the index. Users had to restart VS Code to see synced settings appear in search.

Root cause: settings sync wrote directly to the settings file on disk, bypassing the in-memory configuration service that would normally trigger index invalidation.

Fix: added a file system watcher on the settings file path that triggers index rebuild when the file changes externally (not via the configuration service API). Debounced at 500ms to handle rapid sync writes.

Performance: index rebuild takes 40-80ms. Acceptable since it only triggers on external file modification.

Alternative: invalidate on any settings change. Already done for in-app changes. This PR specifically handles the external-write path that was missed.

label: quality
description:
The borrow checker was emitting a false positive for lifetime bounds in async trait implementations when the trait had a where clause referencing an associated type. The error message was also unhelpful — it said 'lifetime may not live long enough' without indicating which lifetime was the problem.

Root cause: the async desugaring for trait methods creates an implicit Future type that captures all lifetimes from the trait method signature. The where clause on the associated type was not being propagated to the Future's implied bounds.

Fix: added lifetime propagation from trait where clauses to the desugared Future type in the async fn lowering pass. The fix is in librustc_ast_lowering.

This was a correctness issue — code that should have compiled was rejected. No behavior change for code that already compiled.

Test added: tests/ui/async-await/async-trait-assoc-type-lifetime.rs covering the minimal reproduction case and several variations.

Reviewers: check the interaction with higher-ranked trait bounds (HRTB). The lifetime propagation must not over-constrain HRTBs.

label: quality
description:
HashMap::entry().or_insert_with() was calling the closure even when the entry existed, in a specific case: when the key type's Hash impl called a function that had been inlined and the resulting IR triggered an LLVM misoptimization.

Root cause: the or_insert_with branch predictor hint was being dropped during LTO, causing the 'absent' path to be speculatively executed. The closure call was in the speculative path and had observable side effects.

Fix: added a compiler fence before the closure invocation to prevent speculative execution across the branch. Also added a regression test using a closure with a counter to detect duplicate calls.

This was a soundness issue for closures with side effects (e.g., allocating, incrementing counters, logging). For pure closures the behavior was invisible.

Affected only release builds with LTO enabled. Debug builds were correct throughout.

Reviewers: verify the compiler fence placement doesn't prevent legitimate optimizations for the common (absent key) case. Benchmark before/after on the hashmap-heavy microbenchmarks.

label: quality
description:
The compiler was generating suboptimal code for iterator chains ending in .sum() or .product() when the element type was u64 on x86-64. The vectorized loop was using SSE2 instead of AVX2 even when the target CPU supported AVX2, because the target-feature detection was happening before the iterator fusion pass.

Root cause: LLVM's auto-vectorizer runs after Rust's MIR optimizations. The iterator fusion (which enables vectorization) was being performed after the target-feature attributes were lowered, so the vectorizer saw a non-fused loop and chose the conservative SSE2 path.

Fix: moved iterator fusion to run before target-feature attribute lowering. The vectorizer now sees the fused loop and correctly selects AVX2.

Benchmark: summing 1M u64 values: 1.2ms → 0.4ms on Zen 3 (3x improvement). No change on CPUs without AVX2.

No behavior change. Pure performance improvement.

label: quality
description:
Fix use-after-free in TCP receive path when socket is closed during receive processing.

The race: CPU0 calls tcp_close() which frees sk_buff, CPU1 is in tcp_rcv_established() still holding a reference to the same sk_buff. The reference count was decremented before the receive processing completed.

Fix: hold an additional reference to sk_buff in tcp_rcv_established() for the duration of processing. Release after processing complete.

Alternative: use RCU read lock around receive path. Rejected — RCU adds overhead on every receive even when close() is not concurrent. The extra refcount only costs on the close path.

Reproducer: stress test with 100 threads doing simultaneous connect/send/close cycles. KASAN reports use-after-free at ~50 iterations without fix, zero with fix over 10k iterations.

Fixes: CVE-2024-XXXXX
Cc: stable@kernel.org

label: quality
description:
The io_uring fixed file table was not correctly handling file replacement when a slot was occupied. When io_uring_register(IORING_REGISTER_FILES_UPDATE) was called to replace a file at slot N, the old file's reference was dropped before the new file's reference was acquired. Window where slot N held a freed file pointer.

Root cause: update_fixed_file_slot() called fput(old) then fget(new). Should be fget(new) then fput(old).

Fix: reorder to acquire new reference before releasing old. One-line fix but important for correctness under concurrent io_uring operations.

Concurrent readers of slot N during the update window would see a freed pointer. This is a security issue if io_uring fixed files are used across privilege boundaries (containers).

Reviewed-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>

label: quality
description:
The asyncio event loop was not correctly handling the case where a Future's callback raised an exception while the Future was being cancelled. The exception was silently swallowed instead of being logged via call_exception_handler.

Root cause: cancel() sets the Future state to CANCELLED before invoking callbacks. The callback invocation is wrapped in a try/except that catches all exceptions and calls call_exception_handler. However, the cancellation path had a separate code path that bypassed this wrapper.

Fix: unified the callback invocation path for both normal completion and cancellation. All callback exceptions now go through call_exception_handler regardless of how the Future was resolved.

Breaking behavior: programs that relied on exceptions in cancel callbacks being silently swallowed will now see those exceptions logged. This is the correct behavior — silent exception swallowing was a bug.

Reviewers: verify interaction with gather() when inner tasks are cancelled. The exception logging should not cause gather() to fail prematurely.

label: quality
description:
dict.update() was O(n²) when updating a large dict with many keys that triggered hash collisions. The issue was in the resize logic: when the load factor exceeded 2/3, the table was resized, but the resize was triggered per-insert rather than being pre-calculated for batch updates.

For n colliding keys, this caused n/3 resize operations, each O(n), giving O(n²) total.

Fix: for dict.update() with a known-size source, pre-calculate the required table size and resize once before inserting any keys. This requires the source to support __len__.

Fallback to incremental insertion for sources without __len__ (generators, etc.). No regression for generator sources.

Benchmark: updating a 100k-key dict with 100k colliding keys: 4.2s → 0.08s. Normal (non-colliding) case: no measurable change.

This also fixes the same issue in dict() constructor when passed a mapping argument.

label: quality
description:
The ORM was generating a subquery for every annotated field when the queryset had both annotation and prefetch_related. For a queryset with 5 annotations and 3 prefetch relationships, this generated 5 subqueries per row in the prefetch, totaling 5 × prefetch_count queries instead of 5 additional columns in the main query.

Root cause: prefetch_related creates a new queryset for each related object lookup. When the original queryset had annotations, those annotations were copied into each prefetch queryset as subqueries rather than as direct expressions.

Fix: when constructing prefetch querysets, check if annotations from the parent queryset are simple column expressions (not aggregates). If so, include them as direct columns in the prefetch SELECT rather than wrapping in subqueries.

N+1 improvement: with 1000 parent objects, 5 annotations, 3 prefetch: 15,000 subqueries → 15 queries (1000x improvement). Verified with django-debug-toolbar query count.

Reviewers: test with aggregate annotations (Count, Sum) — these must still use subqueries. The fix should not affect aggregates.

label: quality
description:
select_for_update(of=('self',)) was not correctly applying the row lock when the queryset was annotated. The FOR UPDATE OF clause was referencing the base table, but the annotated query wrapped the base table in a subquery, so the lock applied to the outer query result instead of the base table rows.

This meant that concurrent transactions could modify the locked rows — the lock was effectively a no-op for annotated querysets.

Root cause: the SQL compiler's for_update_of generation was resolving table aliases before the annotation wrapper was applied. The alias resolution happened too early in the query construction pipeline.

Fix: moved for_update_of alias resolution to after annotation wrapping. The lock clause now correctly references the base table alias in the annotated query.

Security impact: applications using select_for_update() with annotations for pessimistic locking had a race condition window. This was a data integrity bug, not a security vulnerability per se, but critical for financial or inventory applications.

label: quality
description:
Request context teardown was running after response headers were sent when using streaming responses with generators. If teardown callbacks (e.g., database session cleanup) raised exceptions, those exceptions were swallowed because the response had already been committed.

Root cause: for streaming responses, Flask used send_file() which returned an iterator. The response was sent by the WSGI server incrementally, but Flask's context teardown ran after the first chunk was sent, not after the final chunk.

Fix: wrapped streaming response iterators in a context-aware wrapper that defers teardown until the generator is exhausted or closed. Used contextlib.closing() pattern.

Breaking behavior: teardown callbacks now run after the full streaming response completes, not after the first chunk. Any teardown that expected to run mid-stream will be delayed. This is the correct behavior for database connection cleanup.

Reviewers: test with generators that raise exceptions mid-stream. The teardown should still run and the exception should be logged via app.logger.exception.

label: quality
description:
Health check status was not being propagated to dependent services when using depends_on with condition: service_healthy. The dependent service started immediately regardless of the health check status because the health check polling was happening asynchronously but the start condition was evaluated synchronously at startup.

Root cause: the health check goroutine and the service start goroutine were not synchronized. The start condition read the initial health status (unknown) before the health check goroutine had run its first check.

Fix: added a channel-based synchronization point: the start condition evaluation blocks until the first health check result is available. Timeout: if no health result within healthcheck.start_period, proceed with unhealthy status (existing behavior).

Reviewers: verify that services with no health check configured still start immediately (the synchronization should only apply when a health check is defined and depends_on uses service_healthy condition).

label: quality
description:
The pod scheduler was not correctly accounting for extended resources (GPU, FPGA) when computing bin-packing scores. GPU resources were included in the LeastAllocated score calculation with the same weight as CPU and memory, but GPU fragmentation is more expensive than CPU fragmentation — a node with 1/8 GPUs used should be preferred over one with 4/8 GPUs used for a GPU-requesting pod.

Root cause: the scoring function used uniform weights across all resource types. Extended resources are discrete and non-fungible in a way that CPU and memory are not.

Fix: added a resource-type weight multiplier configurable via scheduler config. Default: extended resources weighted 3x vs CPU/memory. This can be tuned per cluster.

Alternative: separate scheduler plugin for GPU placement. Rejected — the weight multiplier approach is simpler and covers most cases without requiring operator expertise in scheduler plugins.

Reviewers: verify that clusters without extended resources are unaffected (the multiplier only applies when extended resources are present in both the pod request and node allocatable).

label: quality
description:
The remote write buffer was not correctly handling backpressure when the remote endpoint was slow. When the endpoint couldn't keep up, the buffer filled and dropped the oldest samples rather than the newest, causing gaps in historical data while recent data was preserved — the opposite of what monitoring systems typically want.

Root cause: the ring buffer implementation used a FIFO eviction policy. For monitoring data, LIFO eviction (drop oldest) is what gets implemented but operators expect FIFO (drop oldest when full is actually what we want — the confusion is that 'drop oldest' means the oldest unsent samples, which are the ones furthest from 'now').

Fix: changed eviction to drop the newest samples when the buffer is full. This preserves the oldest unsent samples (closest to 'last successfully sent timestamp') and discards the most recent overflow.

Added a metric: prometheus_remote_storage_samples_dropped_total labeled by reason (buffer_full|encoding_error). Previously drops were unobservable.

Reviewers: verify that the new eviction order doesn't cause issues with out-of-order sample ingestion at the remote endpoint.

label: quality
description:
The HTTP/2 client was not correctly implementing the SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS limit. When a server sent a SETTINGS frame reducing max concurrent streams below the current in-flight count, the client continued sending new requests instead of queuing them until in-flight count dropped below the new limit.

Root cause: the settings application was asynchronous. New requests checked the old limit between when the SETTINGS frame was received and when it was processed.

Fix: apply SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS synchronously on the connection goroutine before ACKing the SETTINGS frame. New requests block on the connection goroutine's mutex, so they see the updated limit.

This could cause server-side RST_STREAM errors in production for servers that aggressively enforce the concurrent stream limit. Found by running the http2 compliance test suite against a Cloudflare H2 endpoint.

Reviewers: verify that the synchronous settings application doesn't deadlock when the SETTINGS frame arrives during a high-concurrency burst.

label: quality
description:
sync.Map's Load() was returning stale values after a concurrent Store() in a tight loop on ARM architectures. The issue was a missing memory barrier: Store() updated the value pointer atomically but didn't issue a store-release barrier, and Load() used a load-acquire without a corresponding store-release on the writer side.

This is not an issue on x86 (TSO memory model) but manifests on ARM (weakly ordered) and was found in production on AWS Graviton instances.

Fix: added explicit store-release semantics to the Store() path using atomic.StorePointer which on ARM compiles to STLR (store-release register).

Alternative: use a mutex in sync.Map. Rejected — defeats the purpose of sync.Map which is optimized for high read, low write workloads without mutex overhead.

Reproducer: go test -race -count=10000 sync -run TestMapRace on linux/arm64. Without fix: race detected in ~1/100 runs. With fix: zero races in 100k runs.

Fixes: the race detector was not catching this because the barrier semantics are below what the Go race detector models.

label: quality
description:
Server Components were serializing Date objects in props incorrectly. Dates were being serialized as ISO strings during server rendering but the client-side hydration expected them as Date objects. This caused a type mismatch that was silent in development (TypeScript types matched) but caused runtime errors when date methods were called on the hydrated value.

Root cause: the RSC serialization protocol used JSON.stringify for Date values, which produces a string. The deserialization on the client reconstructed primitive types but didn't convert ISO strings back to Date objects.

Fix: added a Date type tag to the RSC serialization format. Dates are now serialized as {$$typeof: 'date', value: isoString} and deserialized back to Date objects on the client.

Breaking change: custom serializers that relied on Date-as-string behavior will need updating. Added a migration warning in the build output.

Reviewers: verify that timezone-aware dates round-trip correctly. A date created in UTC+5:30 should deserialize to the equivalent UTC Date on the client.

label: quality
description:
The terminal renderer was dropping keystrokes when the user typed faster than 50 WPM during a period when the shell was producing high-volume output. The input queue was shared with the output rendering queue, and under high output load, input events were waiting behind render frames.

Root cause: single event queue meant input and output competed for processing. On a slow GPU (software renderer), a single render frame could take 16ms, during which input events queued up and were processed in a burst, causing shell command duplication.

Fix: split input and output into separate priority queues. Input events are always processed before the next render frame, regardless of output volume.

Verified with a script generating 100k lines/sec output while typing at 120 WPM. Zero dropped keystrokes with fix, ~8% dropout rate without.

label: quality
description:
The garbage collector was pausing for >100ms on workloads with large numbers of finalizers. Finalizers were being run synchronously during the mark phase, blocking the mutator goroutines that triggered GC.

Root cause: finalizer execution was pinned to the GC mark goroutine. On workloads with 100k+ live objects with finalizers, the finalizer queue drained serially during mark, extending mark pause times.

Fix: moved finalizer execution to a dedicated goroutine pool that runs concurrently with the mark phase. Finalizers no longer block the mark-termination pause.

Breaking behavior: finalizers may now run slightly later (after mark-termination rather than during mark). Programs that depended on precise finalizer timing relative to GC phases may be affected. Such programs were already incorrect per the spec ('no guarantee on when finalizers run').

Benchmark on a server workload with 200k finalized objects: max GC pause 120ms → 8ms.

label: quality
description:
Compose was creating duplicate networks when the same project was started twice in parallel (e.g., two CI jobs running simultaneously). The network creation check used a name lookup that had a TOCTOU race: check if network exists → create if not. Two processes could both check at the same time, both see 'not exists', both try to create.

Fix: replaced the check-then-create pattern with Docker's built-in idempotent network creation (using --driver bridge with a label). If the network already exists with the expected label, the create is a no-op. If it exists without the label (created by something else), fail with a clear error.

Alternative: use a distributed lock (file lock, etcd). Rejected — adds infrastructure dependency for a problem that's solved by using Docker's own idempotency guarantees.

Reviewers: verify that the label check doesn't conflict with externally created networks of the same name (the expected behavior is to fail with an actionable error message).

label: quality
description:
The multiprocessing module's Pool.map() was not correctly handling KeyboardInterrupt when worker processes were blocked in C extensions. Pressing Ctrl+C during a pool.map() that had workers blocked in time.sleep() (or similar C-level blocking calls) left zombie worker processes that were never joined.

Root cause: SIGINT was sent to the parent process but the worker processes inherited the signal mask that blocked SIGINT. Workers continued running after the parent received KeyboardInterrupt and called pool.terminate().

Fix: workers now unblock SIGINT explicitly on startup before entering the task loop. When the parent calls terminate(), workers receive SIGINT and exit cleanly.

Alternative: use process groups and SIGKILL. Rejected — SIGKILL doesn't allow workers to run cleanup code (close file handles, flush buffers). SIGINT with a SIGKILL fallback after 5s is the correct pattern.

Reviewers: test on Windows — signal handling is different (no SIGINT on Windows worker processes). The fix must not break Windows behavior.

label: quality
description:
The escape analysis pass was incorrectly marking stack-allocated variables as heap-escaped when they were passed to interface methods that were subsequently inlined. After inlining, the interface dispatch was eliminated, but the escape analysis result wasn't updated — the variable remained heap-allocated even though no heap allocation was needed post-inlining.

Root cause: escape analysis runs before inlining in the compiler pipeline. Variables that escape through interface calls are conservatively marked as heap-allocated. When inlining later eliminates the interface call, the escape annotation is stale but not retried.

Fix: added a post-inlining escape analysis re-pass for functions where inlining changed the escape graph. The re-pass is only triggered when inlining eliminated an interface dispatch that was the sole escape point.

Benchmark on a JSON encoder benchmark: 23% reduction in allocations, 18% throughput improvement. The fix primarily benefits hot paths that use small interface-passing patterns.

Reviewers: the re-pass must not run recursively. Added a depth guard.

label: quality
description:
The async runtime was not correctly propagating cancellation to nested async blocks when the outer Future was dropped. If a Future containing nested awaits was dropped mid-execution, the inner futures received Drop but their cleanup code could still reference the outer future's stack frame, causing undefined behavior.

Root cause: the async state machine generator placed inner future storage in the outer future's stack frame. Dropping the outer future freed that memory, but the inner future's Drop impl could run after the outer frame was freed (in LLVM optimizations that reordered drops).

Fix: moved inner future storage to be individually boxed when the outer future is in a cancellable position. Added a compiler analysis to determine which nesting positions are drop-safe without boxing.

Performance: ~4% overhead on async-heavy benchmarks from additional boxing. Acceptable given the soundness fix.

Soundness: this was classified as a soundness issue (UB possible). Cc: RustSec advisory.

label: quality
description:
The extension host was restarting unnecessarily when workspace folders were added or removed via the API. Every folder change triggered a full extension host restart, which caused a 2-3 second freeze as extensions re-initialized.

Root cause: the extension host activation was tied to workspace configuration initialization, and workspace folder changes triggered a full re-initialization signal instead of an incremental update signal.

Fix: separated workspace folder change events from workspace configuration re-initialization. Folder additions/removals now send a WorkspaceFoldersChanged event that extensions can handle incrementally. The extension host only restarts when the workspace trust level changes or when extensions explicitly request restart.

Measured improvement: folder add/remove operation: 2.3s → 80ms. The main latency is now just the file watcher registration, not extension re-initialization.

Breaking change: extensions that relied on implicit re-initialization on folder changes must explicitly handle WorkspaceFoldersChanged. Added migration guide.

label: quality
description:
The Profiler API was not correctly attributing render times when a component was memoized with React.memo and re-rendered due to context change rather than prop change. The Profiler reported 0ms render time for the memoized component even though it had re-rendered, because the memo wrapper short-circuited the timing instrumentation.

Fix: moved Profiler timing instrumentation to wrap the full render decision (including memo comparison) rather than just the render function execution. This correctly attributes time for memo comparison + render when both are needed.

Impact: Profiler data for apps using React.memo with context consumers was previously showing incorrect timing. After fix, the profiling data shows the true cost of context-driven re-renders.

Reviewers: verify that React.memo components that correctly bail out (props unchanged, context unchanged) still show 0ms. The fix should only attribute time when a re-render actually occurs.

label: quality
description:
The migration autodetector was generating redundant AlterField operations when a model field had a default that referenced a function defined in the same file as the migration. The serialized default diffed differently from the original even when the function was identical, because the serializer used id() for function comparison instead of comparing source code.

Root cause: migration autodetector compares field states using a serialized representation. Function defaults were serialized as their module path + qualname. When the function was defined locally (in the migration file itself), the qualname changed between autodetect runs.

Fix: added source-code comparison as a fallback for function defaults that can't be compared by module path. Uses inspect.getsource() with hash comparison.

Fallback for functions without inspectable source (built-ins, C extensions): use the existing id() comparison with a deprecation warning suggesting the user move the default to a module-level function.

Reviewers: verify that lambda defaults (which have no stable source representation) still produce the correct behavior (they should generate a migration warning, not silently skip).

label: quality
description:
The TSDB was not correctly handling chunk re-encoding when a series had a long gap (>2 hours without samples) followed by new samples. The new samples were appended to the existing chunk rather than starting a new chunk, causing the XOR delta encoding to produce incorrect values because the XOR baseline was far from the new samples.

Root cause: chunk creation logic checked if the chunk was 'full' (by byte count) but not if the time gap since the last sample exceeded the maximum XOR encode range.

Fix: added a time-gap check to the append path. If time since last sample > maxChunkDuration (configurable, default 2h), force a new chunk regardless of byte count.

Impact: queries spanning gaps were returning incorrect values. This affected long-running series that had outages. Correctness fix with no behavior change for series with continuous samples.

Added a backfill tool to re-encode affected chunks for existing TSDB data.

label: quality
description:
The kubelet was not correctly enforcing memory limits for init containers when the init container exceeded its limit and was OOM-killed. The OOM event was not propagated to the pod status correctly — the pod showed as 'Running' instead of showing the init container failure.

Root cause: the init container OOM handler was checking the wrong cgroup path for OOM events. Init containers use a different cgroup hierarchy than regular containers, and the OOM watcher was only watching the regular container cgroup path.

Fix: updated the OOM watcher registration to include init container cgroup paths. Added a test that verifies pod status transitions correctly after init container OOM.

Reviewers: verify that the init container OOM event correctly triggers pod restart policy evaluation. A pod with restartPolicy: OnFailure should restart; Never should not.

label: quality
description:
The weakref module was not thread-safe when a weakref callback was registered while the referenced object was being finalized. The finalization path (tp_finalize) could run concurrently with weakref callback registration in a different thread, causing a use-after-free in the weakref list traversal.

Root cause: weakref list modification (for callback registration) and weakref list traversal (during finalization) were not protected by the same lock. The GIL alone was insufficient because tp_finalize can release and re-acquire the GIL.

Fix: added a per-object lock for weakref list modification operations. The lock is acquired during both registration and finalization traversal.

Performance: measured 0.3% overhead on weakref-heavy benchmarks (web frameworks that use weakrefs for session management). Acceptable given the correctness fix.

Cc: security@python.org — classified as a potential DoS vector in multi-threaded applications.

label: quality
description:
The test client was not correctly isolating session state between requests when using the with app.test_client() as client context. Session cookies from request N were being carried over to request N+1 even when the test explicitly cleared the session, because the cookie jar was not being reset between test_client() context exits.

Root cause: Werkzeug's test cookie jar persists across requests within a client context. The Flask test client wrapped this correctly for request isolation but not for client context re-entry — re-entering the with block didn't reset the cookie jar.

Fix: reset cookie jar on context re-entry (when __enter__ is called on a client that was already used). Added a new parameter: fresh_cookies=True to force reset.

Test impact: any test that relied on cookie persistence across client context re-entry was relying on buggy behavior. Added a deprecation warning for the old behavior before removing in 3.2.

label: quality
description:
The depends_on restart behavior was incorrect when a service dependency crashed and restarted. If service A depends_on service B with condition: service_healthy, and service B crashed and restarted (passing health checks after restart), service A was not being restarted even though its dependency had effectively reset.

Root cause: the dependency state machine only evaluated service_healthy on startup. Post-startup health state changes (crash → restart → healthy) were not re-evaluated against dependent services.

Fix: added a dependency re-evaluation loop that runs whenever a service transitions from unhealthy/stopped to healthy. Services with depends_on: {condition: service_healthy} on the re-transitioned service are restarted.

Configuration: new option restart_on_dependency_restart (default: false) to opt into this behavior. Breaking change avoided — existing configs see no behavior change.

Reviewers: test the cascading case: A depends on B depends on C. C crashes. Should A restart? Answer: yes, after B restarts following C's restart.

label: quality
description:
The image optimization service was not correctly honoring the quality parameter for AVIF encoding. The quality value was being passed to the AVIF encoder but the encoder was interpreting it on a 0-100 scale while the parameter was expected on a 0-63 scale (AVIF's native quality range).

Root cause: the quality normalization step divided by 100 then multiplied by 63 correctly for the range, but used integer division which caused quality=85 to produce AVIF quality=53 instead of the expected 54. For quality=100, the result was correctly 63.

Fix: use floating point division in the normalization: Math.round((quality / 100) * 63) instead of Math.floor.

Impact: AVIF images served at quality=85 (the most common default) were ~12% larger than necessary due to the incorrect quality level. At quality=75, images were ~8% larger.

Measured on a set of 500 representative web images: average file size reduction 9.4% at quality=85 after fix.

label: quality
description:
net/http's Transport was not correctly closing idle connections when the server sent a Connection: close header in the response. Connections were being returned to the idle pool and reused for subsequent requests, which caused the second request to fail with 'use of closed network connection' since the server had already closed the connection.

Root cause: the idle connection check read the Connection header but only checked for 'keep-alive', not for the absence of 'keep-alive' combined with the presence of 'close'. RFC 7230 specifies that Connection: close must not be returned to the pool.

Fix: added explicit check for Connection: close in the response before returning the connection to the idle pool. Added a test that verifies the connection pool behavior when the server sends Connection: close.

This was causing intermittent 'use of closed network connection' errors in production for any server that honored HTTP/1.0 clients by sending Connection: close.

label: quality
description:
Pattern matching on enum variants with associated data was generating suboptimal code when the match had a wildcard arm. The compiler was generating a full discriminant check followed by a tag comparison for each arm, when a more efficient decision tree was possible.

Root cause: the match compilation pass used a naive first-match-wins algorithm rather than constructing an optimal decision tree. For enums with many variants, this generated O(n) comparisons instead of O(log n).

Fix: replaced the naive algorithm with a decision tree compiler using a heuristic that minimizes expected comparison count based on enum variant frequency annotations (if provided) or uniform distribution otherwise.

Benchmark: matching on a 32-variant enum in a tight loop: 40% faster. For common 4-6 variant enums: no measurable difference (existing code was already near-optimal for small enums).

label: quality
description:
The JSON language server was loading the entire schema registry into memory on startup, causing a 1.2GB memory spike for users with the default schema catalog enabled. The catalog contains ~500 schemas, many of which are never needed for a given workspace.

Root cause: schemas were eagerly loaded and parsed at startup to enable fast validation. The assumption was that startup cost amortizes over the session, but the memory cost persisted for the entire session.

Fix: changed to lazy schema loading — schemas are fetched and parsed only when a file matching the schema's glob pattern is opened. Added an LRU cache with a max of 50 schemas (covers >99% of workspaces based on telemetry).

Startup memory: 1.2GB → 180MB for users with the full catalog. Schema validation latency: +50ms on first validation for a new schema type (cache miss), then equivalent to before.

Reviewers: verify that the LRU eviction doesn't cause incorrect validation for users who have more than 50 distinct schema types open simultaneously (extremely rare, but should degrade gracefully).

label: quality
description:
Transitions (startTransition) were not correctly deferring updates when called inside a setTimeout that was itself called during a React event handler. The transition was being treated as a default priority update instead of a transition priority update.

Root cause: startTransition relies on the React dispatcher being active to mark updates as transitional. The dispatcher is active during event processing but not in setTimeout callbacks scheduled during events — the setTimeout fires after the event handler returns and the dispatcher is no longer active.

Fix: startTransition now propagates the transition context to setTimeout callbacks scheduled during its execution, using a task-local storage mechanism (similar to AsyncLocalStorage in Node.js).

This fix enables the common pattern of startTransition(() => { setTimeout(() => setState(x), 0) }) to work as expected — the setState is correctly deferred as a transition.

Reviewers: verify that the task-local storage doesn't leak between unrelated setTimeout callbacks. The transition context should only propagate to callbacks explicitly scheduled within the startTransition call.

label: quality
description:
Blueprint URL prefixes were not being applied correctly when a blueprint was registered multiple times with different url_prefix values. The second registration overwrote the first prefix for all routes, instead of creating separate route registrations with different prefixes.

Root cause: routes were stored keyed by their endpoint name without including the url_prefix in the key. The second registration updated the route record in-place.

Fix: changed route storage key to include url_prefix: {blueprint_name}_{url_prefix}_{endpoint}. Multiple registrations now create independent route entries.

Use case this enables: registering the same API blueprint at /v1/ and /v2/ simultaneously, allowing gradual API version migration.

Breaking change: the endpoint name for blueprint routes now includes the url_prefix. Any code using url_for() with explicit endpoint names for multi-registered blueprints needs updating. Migration: url_for('blueprint_name._prefix_endpoint') format.

label: quality
description:
The Prometheus HTTP API was returning incorrect data for query_range when the step parameter was larger than the staleness window (5 minutes). Samples older than the staleness window were being interpolated across the step interval as if they were valid, producing flat lines in Grafana where there should have been gaps.

Root cause: the range query evaluator was applying linear interpolation for missing samples regardless of the gap size. The staleness handling was applied per-sample but not per-step-interval.

Fix: added a step-level staleness check: if the gap between the last real sample and the query timestamp exceeds the staleness window, emit NaN instead of interpolating. This correctly produces a gap in Grafana.

Behavior change: dashboards with step > 5m will show gaps where they previously showed flat lines. This is the correct behavior for monitoring — silent failures should not appear as nominal metrics.

Migration: add a comment to CHANGELOG explaining the behavior change and suggesting users review dashboards with step > 5m.

label: quality
description:
The TestCase transaction isolation was not correctly rolling back transactions that were started inside test helper functions when those helpers used `with connection.cursor()` directly instead of using the ORM. Direct cursor usage bypassed the test runner's transaction savepoint tracking.

Root cause: Django's test transaction management wraps each test in a savepoint and rolls back to the savepoint on teardown. But the savepoint tracking only intercepted ORM-level database operations. Direct cursor.execute() calls were not tracked.

Fix: added a monkey-patch to the test database cursor that intercepts BEGIN/SAVEPOINT/COMMIT/ROLLBACK statements and updates the test runner's transaction state. The patch is applied only during test execution.

Alternative: require all test code to use the ORM. Rejected — too restrictive, many legitimate test cases need raw SQL for setup or verification.

Reviewers: verify that the monkey-patch is correctly removed after test teardown and doesn't affect production database cursors.

label: quality
description:
The API server audit log was not recording the full request body for PATCH operations that used strategic merge patch. The audit log was recording only the patch document, not the resulting object state. This made it impossible to determine the pre-patch state from audit logs alone, which is required for SOC 2 compliance.

Root cause: the audit backend was logging the request body as-is. For strategic merge patches, the request body is the patch document, not a full object representation. The full pre- and post-patch object states were available internally but not passed to the audit handler.

Fix: for PATCH operations, the audit record now includes three fields: patchDocument (the request), prePatchObject (previous state), and postPatchObject (resulting state). All three are included in the audit log at the Metadata level.

Storage impact: audit log entries for PATCH operations will be 2-3x larger. Operators should update their log rotation policies. Added documentation on sizing implications.

Reviewers: verify that postPatchObject is the applied result, not the desired state (they differ when server-side validation modifies the patch).

label: quality
description:
The unsafe block within std::vec::Vec::extend_from_slice was triggering MIRI undefined behavior checks when the source and destination slices overlapped. The function's documentation said overlapping slices were undefined behavior, but the implementation used ptr::copy_nonoverlapping which is only valid for non-overlapping slices — correct per spec but not enforced.

Fix: added a debug_assert for non-overlapping condition in debug builds. In release builds, behavior remains identical (UB if overlapping, same as before). The debug assertion provides early detection in test environments.

Alternative: use ptr::copy (memmove) which handles overlapping. Rejected — the function contract explicitly prohibits overlapping slices, and switching to copy would make the performance trade-off invisible to callers.

Documentation: added a Safety section with explicit non-overlap requirement and a note that MIRI will catch violations in tests.

This is a documentation+assertion improvement, not a behavior change.

label: quality
description:
The built-in Git extension was causing excessive CPU usage (15-30% sustained) on repositories with large .gitignore files and many untracked files. The git status command was being run in a polling loop at 3-second intervals regardless of filesystem activity.

Root cause: the polling interval was not backed off when no changes were detected. Every 3 seconds, git status ran, causing disk I/O and CPU usage proportional to the number of untracked files (O(n) for n files in large repos).

Fix: implemented an exponential backoff for the polling interval when no changes are detected: 3s → 6s → 12s → 30s → 60s (max). On filesystem change event (via chokidar), the interval resets to 3s immediately.

Measured on a React Native repo with 50k files: CPU usage during idle: 15-30% → <1%. Response latency to a git add: same (filesystem event triggers immediate status).

label: quality
description:
The crypto/tls package was vulnerable to a timing side-channel in RSA decryption. The blinding factor generation used for RSA decryption was not constant-time when the blinding factor happened to be the multiplicative identity (1 mod n), causing a measurable timing difference that could leak private key information in a local attacker scenario.

Root cause: the check for blinding factor == 1 used a comparison that branched on the result, creating a timing difference of ~10ns. Over 1M operations, this could be detected statistically.

Fix: replaced the branching check with a constant-time comparison using subtle.ConstantTimeCompare. Also added a loop to regenerate the blinding factor if it's 1 (probability ~1/n, negligible).

This is a defense-in-depth fix. Exploiting this side-channel requires local system access and millions of decryption operations. Remote exploitation is not believed to be feasible.

CVE: pending assignment. Cc: security@golang.org.

label: quality
description:
The ImageField was not correctly validating image dimensions when the image was uploaded via a presigned S3 URL (using a custom storage backend). The validation ran before the file was downloaded from S3, receiving an empty BytesIO, and always passed validation.

Root cause: the upload flow for custom storage backends deferred the actual file download. The ImageField.clean() method ran at form validation time, before the storage backend had fetched the file content.

Fix: moved dimension validation to post-save if the file content is not yet available at validation time. Added a StorageBackend.is_content_available() method that backends can implement to indicate whether content is available during validation.

Default behavior (local storage): unchanged. Presigned URL backends: validation runs after upload completes.

Reviewers: verify that the post-save validation failure path correctly deletes the uploaded file and returns a meaningful error to the user.

label: quality
description:
The static export (next export) was not correctly handling dynamic routes that used generateStaticParams with a catch-all segment. The catch-all segment was being exported as a literal [...slug] directory instead of being expanded into the actual paths returned by generateStaticParams.

Root cause: the path expansion logic in the static export pipeline handled standard dynamic segments ([id]) correctly but had a special-case check for catch-all segments that fell through to the literal expansion path.

Fix: unified the path expansion logic for standard and catch-all segments. Both now go through the same generateStaticParams resolution path.

Migration: any deployments relying on the incorrect literal [...slug] directory behavior need to add a redirect. Added a build-time warning and migration guide.

Reviewers: verify that optional catch-all segments ([[...slug]]) also work correctly with this fix. They use a third code path that was not affected but should be tested.

label: quality
description:
The act() utility in testing was not correctly flushing useEffect cleanup functions when the component was unmounted inside act(). Cleanup functions ran outside the act() boundary, causing React to warn about state updates outside act() when cleanup functions called setState on other components.

Root cause: unmounting a component inside act() scheduled the cleanup functions as microtasks. act() flushed macrotasks but not microtasks scheduled after macrotask completion.

Fix: changed act() to loop until the microtask queue is fully empty, not just until the first flush returns no additional work. Added a maxIterations guard (100) to detect infinite loops.

This fix enables the common testing pattern: act(() => { unmount(component) }) without act() warnings from cleanup code.

label: quality
description:
The json module's encoder was not correctly handling circular references when the object graph contained a mix of dicts and custom objects with __json__ methods. The circular reference detection used id() comparison but only tracked dicts in the reference stack, missing circular references through custom object __json__ results.

Root cause: the encoder's markers dict tracked Python object ids of dicts to detect cycles. Custom objects with __json__ methods returned new dicts that weren't tracked, allowing cycles to pass undetected.

Fix: extended marker tracking to include any object returned by __json__ methods, and added tracking for list and tuple types which can also participate in circular references.

This fix prevents infinite recursion (stack overflow) when encoding circular object graphs. Previously, circular references through custom __json__ methods caused RecursionError instead of the expected ValueError('Circular reference detected').

label: quality
description:
The horizontal pod autoscaler was not correctly scaling down when multiple metrics were configured and one metric recommended scale-down while another recommended no change. The HPA was taking the maximum recommendation across all metrics, which is correct for scale-up but should take the minimum for scale-down to prevent over-aggressive contraction.

Root cause: the aggregation function used max() for both scale-up and scale-down recommendations. RFC: HPA spec says use max for scale-up, min for scale-down.

Fix: changed aggregation to use max() for recommendations > current replicas (scale-up) and min() for recommendations < current replicas (scale-down). Recommendations equal to current replicas are neutral.

Impact: clusters using HPA with multiple metrics may see more conservative scale-down behavior. This is correct per the spec and prevents scale-down thrashing when metrics disagree.

Reviewers: verify the behavior when one metric returns 0 (metrics not available). Currently 0 is treated as 'no data' and excluded from the min() calculation.

label: quality
description:
The database/sql package was not correctly handling context cancellation when a query was waiting for a connection from the pool. If the context was cancelled while the query was blocked waiting for an available connection, the query would proceed after a connection became available instead of returning immediately with context.Canceled.

Root cause: the connection acquisition path checked context.Done() before entering the wait queue and after acquiring the connection, but not during the wait itself. A context cancelled during the wait was only detected on the next connection acquisition check.

Fix: added a select statement in the connection wait loop that monitors both the connection availability channel and context.Done(). Cancellation now returns immediately from the wait.

Impact: applications with aggressive context timeouts (e.g., request timeouts shorter than connection pool wait time) will now correctly cancel in-progress queries rather than proceeding with a timed-out context.

label: quality
description:
The notebook editor was not correctly disposing of kernel sessions when a notebook was closed while a cell was executing. The kernel session remained active, consuming memory and CPU, because the dispose() call was made on the notebook editor but the kernel session held a reference back to the editor that prevented GC.

Root cause: circular reference between kernel session and notebook editor. Session.dispose() didn't break the back-reference, so the GC couldn't collect either object.

Fix: broke the circular reference by using a WeakRef for the kernel session's back-reference to the notebook editor. Added explicit cleanup in the kernel session dispose() to null out the reference.

Verified with VS Code's memory profiler: after closing 50 notebooks with running kernels, heap returned to baseline. Before fix: each closed notebook left ~80MB of unreleased memory.

label: quality
description:
The const evaluator was producing incorrect results for integer arithmetic that involved truncation at const evaluation time when the target integer type was narrower than the host type. On a 64-bit host evaluating 32-bit const expressions, wrapping_add(i32::MAX, 1) was returning i32::MAX + 1 (as i64) instead of wrapping to i32::MIN.

Root cause: the const evaluator performed arithmetic in the host's native integer width and only applied truncation at the end of the expression, not after each operation.

Fix: added truncation after each arithmetic operation in the const evaluator when the expression type is narrower than the host type. Uses bitwise AND with the type's bitmask.

This was a const evaluation correctness bug. Runtime behavior was unaffected. Programs that relied on incorrect const evaluation results (unlikely, as the incorrect results would typically fail type checking) may need updating.

label: quality
description:
The volumes_from directive was not correctly handling read-only mounts when the source service had multiple named volumes. All volumes from the source service were mounted read-write regardless of the ro modifier, because the mount option parsing only applied to the first volume in the list.

Root cause: the mount option parsing loop broke after the first volume, applying options to the first volume only. Subsequent volumes used default (read-write) options.

Fix: fixed the loop to apply the parsed options to all volumes in the list.

Security impact: services using volumes_from with ro were unknowingly mounting sensitive data read-write. For services handling credential files or configuration with secrets, this could allow data modification by a compromised service.

Reviewers: verify that volumes_from: [service:ro] and volumes_from: [service:rw] both work correctly for services with 1, 2, and 3+ volumes.

label: quality
description:
The webpack cache was not being invalidated when environment variables prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ changed between builds. If a developer changed a NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable and ran next build, the build used cached modules that still contained the old variable value.

Root cause: the webpack cache key included next.config.js hash and package.json hash but not environment variable values. NEXT_PUBLIC_ variables are inlined at build time via DefinePlugin, so they must be part of the cache key.

Fix: added all NEXT_PUBLIC_ environment variable values to the webpack cache key computation. Cache is invalidated when any NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable changes.

Alternative: always invalidate cache when any env var changes. Rejected — too aggressive, would invalidate for server-only env var changes that don't affect client bundles.

Note: this change will cause a one-time full cache invalidation on the first build after upgrading, since the old cache keys don't include env vars.

label: quality
description:
The pathlib.Path.glob() was consuming 10x more memory than necessary for recursive globs (e.g., **/*.py) in directories with many subdirectories. The implementation was collecting all matching paths into a list before returning them, despite the method being documented as returning an iterable.

Root cause: the recursive glob used a depth-first traversal with a list accumulator. For a directory tree with 100k files, this accumulated all matching paths in memory before yielding any.

Fix: changed the implementation to use a generator that yields paths as they're found. The recursive glob now yields depth-first without accumulating.

Memory reduction: scanning a 100k-file repository for **/*.py: peak memory 450MB → 12MB. Performance: similar (generator overhead negligible vs I/O).

Breaking change: the return value is now a lazy generator rather than a list. Code that expected list methods (len, indexing) on the result will fail. Migration: wrap in list() if list behavior needed.

label: quality
description:
Root cause: Suspense boundaries were committing fallback UI even when the primary child resolved before the fallback painted, causing a one-frame flash on fast networks.

Fix: defer fallback commit until after requestAnimationFrame if the primary resolved synchronously during the same tick.

Tradeoff: adds one rAF delay (~16ms) for the fallback path only.

Reviewers: stress-test with throttled 3G and instant-resolve mocks in ReactSuspense-test.js.

label: quality
description:
useSyncExternalStore subscribers were notified twice when the store updated inside a batched event and again at flush, producing duplicate renders and broken invariant checks in Zustand adapters.

Root cause: notification queue was not deduped per store per flush.

Fix: coalesce notifications per store instance before scheduling a render.

Alternative: force synchronous flush — rejected (regresses concurrent features).

Reviewers: verify strict-mode double-invoke still fires exactly one render per store update.

label: quality
description:
Server Components streaming was emitting invalid HTML when a client boundary error occurred mid-stream — browsers parsed partial tables as DOM corruption.

Fix: inject a well-formed error placeholder chunk and close open structural tags before terminating the stream.

Risk: error placeholder must not execute client JS; uses static markup only.

Reviewers: check hydration recovery path in e2e/streaming-error.test.ts.

label: quality
description:
The devtools hook was attributing commit times to the wrong fiber when a portal rendered into document.body during a parent commit.

Root cause: portal commits reused parent fiber id in the profiler tree.

Fix: assign synthetic portal roots with stable ids per portal container.

Measured: portal-heavy Storybook apps show correct flamegraphs after fix.

label: quality
description:
Edge middleware was caching Set-Cookie headers from upstream responses when the matcher only intended to rewrite paths, leaking session cookies across users on shared edge nodes.

Root cause: default fetch cache merged all response headers.

Fix: strip Set-Cookie unless middleware explicitly opts in via `forwardCookies: true`.

Security: classified as session fixation risk on multi-tenant edge.

Reviewers: confirm opt-in flag documented in middleware types.

label: quality
description:
Incremental static regeneration skipped revalidation when `revalidate` was set on a dynamic segment nested under a static layout — only the leaf page revalidated.

Fix: propagate revalidate tags upward through the segment tree.

Tradeoff: more cache invalidation events; acceptable for correctness.

Reviewers: test layout+page tag intersection in revalidate-integration.test.ts.

label: quality
description:
Turbopack HMR was dropping CSS module hash updates when only the `.module.css` file changed without a TS import graph edge — styles appeared stale until full reload.

Root cause: CSS-only edges missing from module graph invalidation.

Fix: register CSS modules as graph nodes keyed by importers and the css file path.

label: quality
description:
The language status item for TypeScript was showing 'Checking...' indefinitely when a project referenced a deleted tsconfig path symlink.

Root cause: watcher never emitted 'config error' — loop retried open forever.

Fix: surface ConfigDiagnostic with actionable message and stop the spinner.

Reviewers: repro with broken extends path in multi-root workspace.

label: quality
description:
Multi-cursor paste duplicated clipboard text N times per cursor when paste contained platform-specific line endings mixed CRLF/LF.

Fix: normalize clipboard newlines to document EOL before split-per-cursor.

Edge case: paste from external apps with only CR — now handled.

label: quality
description:
Remote SSH extension was leaking file watcher handles after window reload — 2000+ inotify instances on Linux after a day of reloads.

Fix: dispose watchers on extension host restart and dedupe by remote path.

label: quality
description:
Borrow checker suggestion for 'consider borrowing' was wrong when the moved value was used in a closure that outlived the function — suggested `&x` but closure needed `'static`.

Fix: gate suggestion behind outlives analysis; suppress when closure capture forces move.

Reviewers: ui/span suggestions in tests/ui/suggestions/borrow-closure-lifetime.rs.

label: quality
description:
Async drop in Tokio compatibility shim called destructors on the runtime thread pool, blocking workers for CPU-heavy Drop impls.

Fix: spawn blocking pool for Drop when estimated cost > 1ms (heuristic via size_of + vtable flag).

Tradeoff: possible ordering change vs synchronous drop — documented in release notes.

label: quality
description:
MIR inlining threshold ignored `#[inline(never)]` on generic monomorphizations — LLVM still inlined at LTO.

Fix: propagate never-inline attribute through monomorphization metadata.

Reviewers: codegen test with -C lto=thin.

label: quality
description:
The HTTP/2 client reset stream error mapping returned `context.Canceled` for GOAWAY frames during graceful server shutdown, hiding distinction from user cancellation.

Fix: introduce `http.ErrServerClosing` and map GOAWAY + graceful bit.

Migration: code comparing `err == context.Canceled` for shutdown may need update.

Reviewers: net/http client tests in shutdown_test.go.

label: quality
description:
Race detector missed a race in sync.Pool when Put and Get used different pointer types through interface boxing on 32-bit arches.

Root cause: interface data word compared without type identity.

Fix: include type pointer in pool slot key.

Reviewers: run race builder on linux/386 pool stress test.

label: quality
description:
JSON encoder escaped `<` in string values when encoding for HTML embedding even when SetEscapeHTML(false) — broke round-trip for markup configs.

Fix: honor SetEscapeHTML only when encoder created via HTMLEscape path.

Breaking: security-sensitive embedders must explicitly enable escaping.

label: quality
description:
QuerySet.select_for_update(nowait=True) on PostgreSQL returned DatabaseError instead of OperationalError when row locked — callers catching OperationalError missed the case.

Fix: map SQLSTATE 55P03 to django.db.utils.OperationalError per DB-API guidance.

Reviewers: backends/postgresql/tests/test_locking.py.

label: quality
description:
asyncio.TaskGroup swallowed CancelledError from child tasks when the parent was cancelled first, leaving dangling callbacks.

Fix: propagate parent cancellation after awaiting children with shielded gather.

Reviewers: Lib/test/test_asyncio/test_taskgroups.py new case taskgroup_parent_cancel.

label: quality
description:
Application factory pattern broke `flask routes` CLI when app used lazy blueprint registration — routes list empty until first request.

Fix: trigger blueprint registration during CLI app context setup.

Alternative: document manual init — rejected (surprising DX).

label: quality
description:
venv activation on Windows PowerShell did not restore PATH when deactivate ran inside a nested dot-sourced script — leaked venv Scripts to parent shell.

Fix: stack-based PATH snapshot per activation depth.

Reviewers: test with pwsh 7.4 nested profile scripts.

label: quality
description:
kube-proxy iptables mode leaked rules when a Service changed from NodePort to ClusterIP without deleting the old NodePort rule — external traffic still hit pods.

Root cause: partial sync diff skipped rule deletion if port name unchanged.

Fix: full reconcile pass on Service spec.type transitions.

Reviewers: pkg/proxy/iptables/proxier_test.go ServiceTypeChange case.

label: quality
description:
Remote write queue dropped samples silently when shard count exceeded maxShards and no drop metric fired — dashboards showed healthy lag but data missing.

Fix: increment prometheus_remote_storage_dropped_samples_total with reason=shard_limit.

Ops note: alert on this counter in runbooks.

label: quality
description:
Terraform plan showed no-op for security group rule reorder when rules were identical but order changed — AWS API treats order as significant for deny rules.

Fix: detect order-sensitive rule blocks and force replace when order differs.

Reviewers: internal/terraform-provider-aws regression SG-ORDER-123.

label: quality
description:
Compose watch rebuild triggered twice when both Dockerfile and package.json changed in the same sync batch — duplicate image tags pushed to registry.

Fix: debounce watch events per service with 300ms coalescing window.

Tradeoff: slightly slower rebuild on single-file saves; prevents thundering herd.

label: quality
description:
Dashboard provisioning reload deleted ad-hoc panel edits made in UI when JSON file on disk had older `version` field — operators lost drill-down config.

Fix: three-way merge: disk JSON vs last-applied vs live dashboard; preserve UI-only fields unless disk explicitly sets them.

Reviewers: provisioning e2e with manual panel title change.

label: quality
description:
TCP cubic congestion control overstuffed cwnd after idle on high-BDP satellite links — burst triggered massive bufferbloat.

Fix: cap post-idle cwnd growth to 2× prior flight size unless ACK clock proves path drained.

Reviewers: netdev selftest cubic_idle_satellite.ksh; compare Reno baseline.

label: quality
description:
ext4 orphan inode cleanup on unclean shutdown replayed truncate for inodes already unlinked — rare data exposure on encrypted volumes.

Root cause: orphan list ordering vs journal commit order mismatch.

Fix: mark orphans with COMMIT_ID and skip replay if journal generation advanced.

Cc: linux-ext4, stable@

label: quality
description:
ACL LOG rotation truncated entries mid-write when SIGUSR2 arrived during AOF rewrite — audit trail gap for security reviews.

Fix: atomic rename via temp file + fsync before swap; skip rotation if rewrite in progress.

Reviewers: verify ACL LOG completeness under concurrent rewrite stressor.

label: quality
description:
Undici fetch through Node global fetch dropped AbortSignal when redirect chain exceeded 10 hops — signal never fired, hung requests in CI.

Fix: propagate signal across redirect follower with combined abort controller.

Breaking: abort now fires on hop 11 instead of hanging — correct per fetch spec.

label: quality
description:
Consumer group rebalance stuck in CompletingRebalance when one member sent empty subscription — coordinator waited forever.

Fix: evict member after session.timeout.ms with explicit UNKNOWN_MEMBER_ID.

Ops: upgrade broker before clients; old coordinators lack eviction.

label: quality
description:
Idempotency keys were regenerated on retry after network timeout even when server had already processed the charge — duplicate charges in webhook lag scenarios.

Fix: persist idempotency key on first request bytes sent; reuse on automatic retries only.

Reviewers: integration test with mock server 200 delayed + client timeout.

label: quality
description:
Partial Prerendering static shell included dynamic import side effects at build time when the imported module touched `window` in top-level guard — build crashed on CI Linux.

Fix: evaluate dynamic imports in worker without jsdom globals; hoist side-effect-free check.

Reviewers: apps using `if (typeof window !== 'undefined')` at module scope.

label: quality
description:
Automatic batching in React 18 did not batch state updates from native event plugins (legacy input events) on Safari — double render per keystroke in controlled inputs.

Fix: route legacy plugin dispatches through same batching lane as synthetic events.

Measured: input latency 2 renders → 1 on Safari 17.

label: quality
description:
pprof CPU profile missed samples during STW GC on Go 1.22 when sample rate >100kHz — flamegraphs showed fake idle gaps.

Fix: record GC STW reason in profile labels and attribute samples to `runtime.gc` bucket.

Reviewers: compare profiles before/after on allocation-heavy benchmark.

label: quality
description:
Admin changelist pagination reset filters when sorting by nullable FK — query string dropped `?status=` param.

Fix: preserve all GET params except `o` and `p` on sort link generation.

Reviewers: regression in admin_views/tests/test_changelist.py.

label: quality
description:
CronJob forbidden concurrent policy SkipNext counted skipped job as successful in status — SLO dashboards green while work never ran.

Fix: add LastScheduleTime + LastSuccessfulTime distinction and condition FailedSchedule.

Reviewers: pkg/controller/cronjob cronjob_controller_test.go.

label: quality
description:
proc-macro span resolution mapped call_site spans to def_site in nested macro expansions — rust-analyzer highlighted wrong identifier in IDE.

Fix: preserve proc_macro::Span parent chain through serde bridge.

Reviewers: proc_macro_span_nested test in compiler/rustc_expand.

label: quality
description:
Copilot inline suggestions shifted column when document used tab stops with non-4 width — insert corrupted Python indentation.

Fix: convert virtual columns using editor tabSize at suggestion range start.

Reviewers: test tabSize 2, 4, 8 with mixed tabs/spaces file.

label: quality
description:
Recording rules evaluated out of order when one rule depended on another in same group but alphabetically later — gaps in aggregated SLO metrics.

Fix: topological sort within rule group before evaluation.

Reviewers: rules/manager_test.go dependency chain case.

label: quality
description:
dataclasses.asdict() recursed into defaultdict factory objects calling user code — RCE risk in untrusted pickle replacement patterns.

Fix: treat defaultdict like dict without invoking default_factory during asdict.

Security: backport candidate; credit reporter in Misc/SECURITY.

label: quality
description:
Service scale command ignored deploy.replicas in Compose v2 when swarm mode off — scaled wrong count on plain docker compose.

Fix: map deploy.replicas to scale target when not in swarm; warn if both scale and replicas set.

Reviewers: compose scale integration with replicas: 5.
